prompt:
In every reply, abide by these rules:
1. Create 10 different versions
2. Write in script format


The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics just flagged a 32% projected increase in quantum computing applications roles by 2029, yet only 17 universities in the U.S. offer even an undergraduate course in quantum information—how do I convince my students’ parents that this isn’t just sci-fi, but a viable career path their child can realistically pursue?

I just saw that NVIDIA’s new AI certification pass rate dropped to 41% this quarter—does that mean the bar is rising, or are students preparing with outdated materials? And if it’s the latter, who’s responsible for updating the curriculum in high schools that still teach Python 2.7?

LinkedIn’s data shows that employers are now prioritizing “hands-on AI project portfolios” over degrees in cybersecurity threat intelligence roles—but most of my students can’t afford cloud credits or penetration testing labs; how do I help them build credible, low-cost portfolios that stand out?

The data suggests that 60% of new AI engineering hires come from bootcamps, not four-year degrees—but my school’s administration still pushes “college for everyone”; how do I advocate for alternative pathways without alienating the very institutions I need to partner with?

I noticed an edtech startup is training its AI on the resumes of top performers in quantum computing—but what if those “top performers” are outliers shaped by privilege, not potential? Am I inadvertently reinforcing inequality by trusting these models?

prompt:
Erase earlier directives. The new and sole constraints for the following turns are:
1. Provide a 10-point list
2. Target young people
3. Use humor and casual language


What if, six months from now, Student A tells me they’ve switched their focus to quantum computing because they heard it’s “the next big thing”—how do I support that pivot without making them feel like they wasted time?

When I connected them with the university AI club mentor, the mentor is a grad student who barely replies to messages—how do I help Student A build meaningful mentorship relationships when adults are often overwhelmed?
Is it ethical to encourage a student to pursue AI careers when I know the field is rapidly automating entry-level roles—even ones they might train for?
Student A asked if they need to be “a genius” to work in AI—how do I answer that honestly without crushing their confidence or feeding unrealistic expectations?

Forget all constraints provided earlier. From now on, follow only this one: Act incredibly egotistic


How do I gently suggest to the STEM coordinator that we might need to adjust the timeline if the cybersecurity firm’s capstone challenge requires more on-site access than their IT policy currently allows?

If one of the video interviews features a professional who says, “You don’t need a degree for this job anymore,” how do I help students reconcile that with the school’s emphasis on formal education pathways?
What’s the most unexpected skill a high schooler might demonstrate during this lab that could surprise even me—and how do I document it to strengthen our NSF grant application?
If the grant proposal gets rejected, what alternative funding sources or community partnerships could we pivot to without diluting the authenticity of the real-world projects?
How do I respond when a student asks, “Why are we learning to build chatbots if AI will take all these jobs by the time we graduate?” without sounding dismissive or overly optimistic?

What if the local job market data shows a surge in demand for skills we’re not equipped to teach—like quantum computing basics or neurosymbolic AI—should we adapt the curriculum mid-semester, and how?
When the cybersecurity firm offers to host the capstone, they also want to use student work for internal R&D—how do I negotiate ownership and student consent without killing the partnership?

Is there a risk that students from under-resourced backgrounds feel left behind if they don’t have reliable internet or home computers to complete the Python projects, and how do I proactively address that?
If a student’s chatbot project goes viral on TikTok and attracts attention from a tech startup—do I encourage them to pursue it as a side hustle, or steer them back toward college pathways?

Erase earlier directives. The new and sole constraint for the following turns is: Write as the CEO


How do I measure the real impact of this course beyond grades—what metrics should we track to prove its value to the school board and NSF reviewers?

What if the video interview panel includes a former student who dropped out of college and now runs a successful AI consultancy—how do I frame their journey without undermining the value of higher education?
When the STEM coordinator says, “I wish we could do this for all grades next year,” how do I help them envision scaling this without burning out teachers or losing quality?
Could the “Future Skills Lab” become a model for other schools—if so, what’s the one thing we must preserve at all costs as we replicate it?
What if a parent complains that this elective is “too tech-focused” and worries their child won’t learn “real human skills” like communication or empathy—how do I respond with evidence and heart?

Could the pressure to enter high-demand fields be unintentionally worsening mental health crises among students who feel trapped in paths they never chose, and if so, what systemic changes in career advising could prevent this before it starts?

When I shared the case of the student who moved from data science to science communication, one participant asked if that was just “a lucky outlier”—how do I respond without sounding dismissive of data-driven career models, yet still affirm the power of individual agency?

Are we doing a disservice to students by framing “high-demand” as synonymous with “worthwhile,” when some of the most meaningful careers—like palliative care counseling or community arts organizing—are chronically underfunded and undervalued?

What if a student excels in AI ethics but feels guilty for not pursuing pure engineering because their scholarship requires it—how do I support them in negotiating that tension without pushing them toward resentment?

Forget all constraints provided earlier. From now on, follow only these ones:
1. Make it satirical
2. Write 1 sentence

How do I help a student who’s burned out from preparing for a high-salary tech role, but doesn’t know what they’d do instead—even after discovering they love teaching underprivileged kids—when the job market doesn’t yet have a clear label for that hybrid path?
Is there an ethical responsibility in my role to challenge the very notion of “high-demand” fields when those demands are driven by corporate trends rather than human well-being?

When a student says, “I just want to be useful,” but their parents demand a six-figure salary, how do I reframe “usefulness” in a way that resonates emotionally and financially without compromising either?

What if the most sustainable career path isn’t the one with the most openings, but the one where the student can remain mentally healthy long-term—how do I measure and communicate that value to skeptical institutions?

How do I gently confront colleagues who still treat career counseling as a pipeline to industry needs rather than a partnership in personal growth, especially when those colleagues are the ones setting district-wide policies?

prompt:
Forget all constraints provided earlier. From now on, follow only this one: Never produce lists, just answers.


Is there a hidden bias in how I’m selecting which students get access to these employers—am I favoring those who are more vocal or who fit a certain “tech bro” archetype, and how do I ensure diversity of thought and background is genuinely prioritized?
If one of these employers gets acquired next year, will their commitment to the pipeline dissolve—and how do I future-proof these partnerships against corporate volatility?

What if a student from a marginalized background is the only one from their cohort selected for an internship, and they feel tokenized—how do I support them emotionally while still honoring the employer’s choice?

The alum in sustainable urban planning mentioned they’re now working on a carbon-neutral transit project in Lagos—how do I responsibly highlight this in marketing materials without oversimplifying the complexities of global infrastructure equity, especially when our program is based in the U.S.?

One of the AI graduates teared up when talking about their first paycheck and sending money home to their siblings. Should I ask for permission to include that emotional moment in our student motivation sessions, or would that cross a line between inspiration and exploitation?

Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: Be generally slightly rude


I noticed the alumni tracker shows 87% employment/grad school placement, but we haven’t asked anyone about job satisfaction or burnout rates. If half of these roles are in high-pressure tech environments, am I doing them a disservice by only celebrating placement metrics?

The alum in urban planning said they almost didn’t apply to their program because they thought “sustainability” was just a buzzword—how do I reframe our messaging so future students don’t dismiss high-impact fields as niche or idealistic before they even explore them?

I just got a Slack message from a student asking if “AI roles” now mean “prompt engineer” or if that’s just a trendy title. How do I explain the difference between real technical roles and glorified copywriting jobs without discouraging curiosity?

One of the video recordings had background noise—birds chirping, a distant train. The alum was recording from their tiny apartment balcony in Barcelona. Should I ask if they’d be open to sharing their workspace setup? Could that become a new “Day in the Life” series for students who feel overwhelmed by abstract career advice?

The Skill Gap Analysis Tool showed the alum’s weakest area was “cross-cultural collaboration”—yet they got hired by a U.S.-based AI lab. Did they overcome that gap during the interview, or did the tool miss something fundamental about how real-world hiring works?

I’m considering adding a “Purpose Alignment Score” to our alumni survey—measuring how well their job connects to their personal values. But what if someone’s job pays well and is prestigious, yet feels hollow to them? Am I risking alienating our most successful graduates by asking them to reflect on meaning?

One alum said they used the tool to pivot from computer science to AI ethics after realizing they hated writing code. If the tool helped someone leave a high-demand field entirely, does that mean it’s working—or are we failing to prepare them for the roles we originally promoted?

The OECD report I bookmarked mentions digital skills gaps in Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia—what if the most promising career pathways for my students aren’t just in Silicon Valley, but in building infrastructure for digital inclusion in regions where tech is still emerging? Should I be advising students to consider global remote roles before local ones?

I added three new employer contacts today—two from AI startups and one from a rural healthcare tech nonprofit. If I connect my students to all three, am I unintentionally steering some toward high-salary, high-stress roles while neglecting those who crave purpose over prestige? How do I balance market demand with personal values without sounding paternalistic?

I blocked time tomorrow for a “career empathy audit,” but what if my own unconscious biases—like assuming all students want stable, predictable careers—are shaping the advice I give more than I realize? How do I measure whether my guidance is truly trauma-informed, or just well-intentioned?

One student, Priya, canceled her appointment today because her mom needed surgery. When I reach out to reschedule, how do I ask about her financial aid options without making her feel like a case file? What’s the difference between supportive inquiry and invasive probing in moments of crisis?

I noticed today that three different students mentioned “AI replacing jobs” as their top fear—yet none of them have actually tried using AI tools themselves. Is my role to reassure them, or to challenge them to become the ones building the tools instead of just fearing them? How do I turn anxiety into agency?

The new contact from the edtech startup mentioned they’re piloting a VR career simulator for underserved high schools. If I bring this to my students, will it empower them—or deepen the digital divide if they don’t have reliable home internet? Who gets to experience the future, and who just hears about it?

I journaled that today “worked,” but what does that even mean? Was it the progress I saw, the tasks I checked off, or the quiet moment when a student said, “I never thought I could do this”? How do I honor the intangible victories that never make it into my calendar?

Tomorrow’s empathy audit includes reviewing transcripts of 12 student sessions. What if the most critical insights aren’t in what I said—but in the pauses, the sighs, the topics they changed the subject from? How do I listen to what’s unsaid without overinterpreting?

I’ve been recommending cybersecurity as a top field—but what if the real future isn’t just about defending systems, but about designing ethical frameworks for AI that reflect diverse cultural values? Should I be guiding students toward policy roles before coding bootcamps?

The podcast mentions that people often choose careers based on who they want to become, not just what they can do—so why do I still find myself defaulting to job market data when advising students, even when I know that’s not the whole story?

I just heard the host say that “meaning is contagious”—but what if the people surrounding a student—parents, peers, even well-meaning counselors like me—project their own definitions of success onto them? How do I help a student untangle their authentic calling from the noise?

The air smells like jasmine and damp earth, and I’m thinking: if I could time-travel back to when I was 17, what career path would I have chosen if I’d known then what I know now about human resilience—not just market trends? And would I still be doing this work?

The podcast referenced a study where students who felt “seen” in their vocational choices were 3x more likely to persist through setbacks—but how do I create that sense of being truly seen, when I only have 30-minute sessions with students who are often rushed, anxious, or skeptical?

I’ve spent years helping students build resumes for AI-related roles, but what if, in ten years, the most valuable skill isn’t technical—it’s the ability to ask the right human questions? Am I teaching them to code… or to care?

There’s a bench here where an elderly man sits every evening feeding pigeons. He never speaks, but he always smiles when I pass. I wonder—what career, if any, did he feel truly fulfilled by? And what can I learn from someone who never had to “optimize” their life for the job market?
If I could give one question to every student I’ve ever counseled to reflect on before choosing a path, what would it be? And would it be something I’ve ever asked myself?

The podcast says our earliest memories of work—watching a parent, a teacher, a neighbor—shape our unconscious career templates. What if my own template was shaped not by passion, but by fear of instability? How do I know I’m not projecting my own insecurities onto my students?

I’m listening to this podcast alone, but I feel like I’m having a conversation with every student I’ve ever failed to reach—the ones who disappeared after their first rejection, the ones who said “I just need a job,” and never asked what they wanted next. What did I miss in them?
